text.title: Quality Attribute Trade-offs
text.summary: Common conflicts between quality attributes, CAP theorem, and stakeholder concern matrix.

rule.statement: Every architectural decision that satisfies a (H,H) scenario must explicitly name which other quality attribute it costs, and by how much. Trade-offs are first-class outputs of QAA.
rule.rationale: Implicit trade-offs become technical debt. Teams optimize availability by sacrificing latency, then are surprised when customers complain about slow responses. Making the trade explicit creates a shared decision log.

rule.statement: When two scenarios conflict, the utility tree priority determines the winner. Document the losing scenario and its concession level in the architecture decision record (ADR).
rule.rationale: Without documented decisions, every new team member re-debates the same trade-offs. The ADR + utility tree provide the rationale that survives team changes.

list.item: Strong consistency vs Performance: ACID DB guarantees correctness but adds lock contention and latency
Availability vs Consistency (CAP): under network partition, choose availability (AP: NoSQL, caches) or consistency (CP: financial transactions)
Microservices vs Performance: independent scaling gains latency cost per service hop
Encryption everywhere vs Performance: TLS + AES adds CPU overhead (typically 5-15%)
Modifiability vs Performance: abstractions (interfaces, adapters) add indirection overhead
Observability vs Performance: structured logging + distributed tracing add per-request overhead
CP (Consistency + Partition tolerance): choose when correctness is non-negotiable — financial transactions, inventory counts. Sacrifice: availability during partition
AP (Availability + Partition tolerance): choose when availability is priority — social feeds, shopping carts, recommendation engines. Sacrifice: immediate consistency (eventual consistency)
CA (Consistency + Availability): only achievable in single-node systems — no network partition tolerance. Not applicable to distributed systems
End Users: Performance, Availability, Usability — they experience latency and downtime directly
Operations: Maintainability, Observability — they carry the pager and deploy the code
Security Team: Security, Compliance, Auditability — they answer to regulators
Business: Scalability, Cost efficiency — they pay the cloud bill and grow revenue
Developers: Modifiability, Testability — they ship features and fix bugs
